Yeah, so I would go back to the project that I was talking about.
And that's mostly around the content generation side of things.
So we already have a lot of users, right, at Coupang.
And we have a lot of vendors that actually give us information on any kind of new products.
That include summaries, insights, any kind of competitor comparisons, those kind of things.
So we need to recommend these products.
And like I said earlier, the product recommendation at Coupang did not have a very good feature store.
And the recommendations actually did not work very well.
Because not fast enough.
And some of them had really bad cold starting problems.
So if I have to talk about the architecture itself, so a lot of ingestion happened from the product information website, PDFs, URLs, etc.
And that mainly found out any 20 to 30 chunks of every kind of product.
And finally, we created a summary on top of the products that kind of have the theme, what is the sentiment, what is the competitive comparison, the executive summary, and all of that.
Basically, a card about the product.
And that is my product level information.
And we actually store it in a separate embedding database after generating the embeddings from it.
Now, coming to the user side of things, I have already mentioned to you about the different kind
Kind of user click-level data that gets actually captured, right?
And that includes interaction with the website, what kind of products they are buying, what kind of vendor site they actually go to.
So these things can be actually leveraged for creating a matrix for the different kind of users.
And then the matrix is actually converted to trees.
And the graph, basically the tree of these users can be used to recommend similar kind of products to new users that kind of use the platform.
Now that we have two different inputs, we have the information about the user and we have the information about the products as well.
So what happens is it becomes very easy to recommend new products to the user.
Now, one of the major challenges that we were facing is these recommendations were not very good looking.
I mean, they are very generic.
I'm looking, you know, they seem very AI-generated, okay?
The product owners actually did not like that.
So what I did is I took this information, I took this data.
So basically I have sequences, right?
And I actually trained one of the distilled models on top of this model.
Basically I fine-tuned that model.
And to do that, I used SageMaker.

And we did it for, you know, we did it for four to five epochs on the entire data itself.
And we had around 100 million records for fine-tuning that.
And the sequence generation or, you know, we actually tracked for publicity of the fine-tuned model.
And I was doing a low-right adaptation of the 5.4 model for around eight layers.
And after doing it, we got almost 19%.
Improvement on top of what we were seeing from the generated product recommendations for the users.

just that we might be facing so that it gives a very clear picture to the leadership of where we are actually, what we are doing.

So at growthx, we generate the long form content for clients, right?
And we want, of course, a reliable way to deliver and measure quality of the content.
And also a way that we can systematically improve it all the time.
So we'd love...
I'd to hear your thoughts and ideas on how would you define quality, or actually, in general, how will you design a system that scores content quality and uses the scores to improve over time?
Okay.
So mainly, I think the challenge is to score the content, right?
Because that will actually drive confidence within the people who are actually consuming it.
So, if I say that you are publishing a lot of articles or generating a lot of articles, and I'm assuming growthx is mainly based around marketing and, you know, the product domains, right?
So it needs to speak the language of the leadership of that particular company.
So I...
I think that makes a very good case for using growthx as their main partner for, you know, content generation and engagement, right?
So one of the main things that needs to be defined are the main quality pillars or, you know, what you would track.
So you look for factuality and accuracy, you look for structure and coherence, and you look for the keyword coverage, basically SEO stuff.
And you also do one more thing, which kind of matches the voice of the brand or, you know, the style of the writing that the brand actually has.
And then you try to find out propensity over here as well, whether the, you know, the generated content is actually going to be interacted a lot or not.
So a model at the end, which kind of tells you what is the likelihood of engagement going to.
For us, I mean, if growthx had to measure their own content.
So those are the, you know, five main pillars that I would define for any kind of, you know, content quality checking.
Now, can you repeat all of them?
Yes.
